title: Social Media Marketing Perth
meta_title: Social Media Marketing Perth | $250 a Month, No Lock-In
meta_description: Social media marketing in Perth for $250 a month, GST inclusive, no lock-in contract. Perth-based, 9 AM to 9 PM AWST, working with clients Australia-wide.
intro: Social media marketing for businesses that know they should be posting and never get to it. $250 a month including GST, no lock-in contract, run from Perth.
body_html:
Posting consistently is a time problem, not a talent problem

The usual pattern looks like this. You set up an Instagram account, post well for three weeks, get busy with the actual work, and the account goes quiet for two months. Then you feel guilty about it, post four things in a single afternoon, and the cycle starts over.

The damage is not that the posts were bad. It is that a page whose last update was in March tells anyone checking that the business might have stopped too. Someone deciding whether to ring you is looking for a sign of life, and an abandoned feed is the wrong signal to send. Fixing that does not take more effort from you. It takes the job coming off your desk and running at a steady, unremarkable pace that does not depend on how busy your week turned out to be.

What social media marketing costs in Perth

OptiTide's social media marketing is $250 a month, in Australian dollars, GST included, with no lock-in contract. It runs month to month, which means it continues because it is worth continuing, not because you signed something twelve months long.

The price is published rather than worked out per enquiry, and the figure you see is the figure you pay: GST is already in it, not added at invoice time. What the work covers for your business is set out on the social media service page and confirmed with you before anything starts, so you are not guessing and neither are we. If you decide to put money behind advertising, that budget goes to the platform and sits outside the monthly figure.

The rest of the pricing works the same way, so you can plan around it:

  • SEO is $750, $1,500 or $2,500 a month, depending on how competitive your market is.
  • Web design is a one-off $750 for Starter or $1,500 for Business, with larger builds quoted individually.
  • Hosting is $25 a month unmanaged or $50 a month managed.

All of it is GST inclusive, none of it is locked in, and you can compare the lot on the services page.

Why consistency beats volume

Cadence matters more than raw volume. A modest number of decent posts, week after week, does more for a small business than a burst of fifteen followed by silence. Part of that is mechanical, in that a dormant account gives a platform nothing recent to put in front of anyone. The rest is simply human. A page that is obviously being kept up says the business is open, active and worth contacting.

The other half of it is the mix. Polished posts show what you sell. Rougher ones, a photo from a job, a short update, a story, show that a real person is behind the account. Both have a job to do, and a plan built only from the polished kind tends to stall, because polish is exactly the part that eats time you do not have.

Which platforms suit which businesses

Being on every platform is a reliable way to be mediocre on all of them. One or two, chosen to match how your customers find and judge you, will beat five neglected accounts.

Instagram

Suits work that is visual or physical: hospitality, retail, salons, fitness, events, and trades with a clear before and after. If what you do photographs well, this is usually the first place to look.

Facebook

Suits businesses selling to households and local neighbourhoods, where recommendations circulate in suburb and community groups. A business page gives people somewhere credible to point when your name comes up.

LinkedIn

Suits selling to other businesses: professional services, B2B suppliers, recruitment, consulting, and firms servicing mining and resources here in Western Australia. Posting volume tends to be lower, and each follower tends to be worth more.

TikTok and short video

Worth considering if you are comfortable on camera or your work is genuinely interesting to watch. Worth skipping if filming would become another job that never gets done, because an empty video account helps nobody.

Social media on its own is rarely the whole answer

Social media is good at keeping you visible to people who already know you exist, and at introducing you to people nearby. It is weaker at catching someone who is searching for exactly what you sell at the moment they want to buy it. That is the job of search engine optimisation, which is why the two sit well beside each other rather than competing for the same budget.

Both also send people to the same destination, so the destination matters. If your website is slow or awkward on a phone, the attention you earn leaks straight back out, and you end up paying to send people somewhere that talks them out of it.

Working with someone based in Perth

OptiTide is Michael James Long trading as OptiTide, ABN 38 163 865 712. Australian owned and operated, based in Perth, Western Australia, and working with clients right across the country, remotely.

Hours are Monday to Friday, 9 AM to 9 PM AWST. The late finish is deliberate. It means you can send feedback in the evening, after you have shut up shop for the day, and review the result the next morning instead of losing a day to the handover. If you are on the east coast, the overlap with your own afternoon is wider than it would be with a nine-to-five.

You also get a client portal. Invoices are there to view and pay, you can track what is happening with your project, and you can message us without digging back through an email chain. Payment is by PayID or bank transfer, Skrill, PayPal or Payoneer.

Getting started

Tell us what your business does, who you want to reach, and which accounts you already have, working or otherwise. You will get a straight answer about whether social media marketing is the right home for $250 a month, including when the honest answer is that something else should come first. Get in touch, or read how we work before you commit to anything.

faqs.q: How much does social media marketing cost in Perth?
faqs.a: OptiTide charges $250 per month for social media marketing, in Australian dollars and GST inclusive, with no lock-in contract. It runs month to month. What the work covers for your business is set out on the social media service page and confirmed with you before anything starts. Any advertising budget you choose to run is separate and goes to the platform.

faqs.q: Am I locked into a contract?
faqs.a: No. Social media marketing runs month to month at $250 including GST, and there is no lock-in contract on any OptiTide service. Web design is a one-off $750 or $1,500, SEO is $750, $1,500 or $2,500 a month, and hosting is $25 or $50 a month, all GST inclusive and none of them locked in.

faqs.q: What gets posted, and how often?
faqs.a: The schedule is agreed with you before we start and then kept to, because consistency week to week is what makes a page look like an open business. A LinkedIn page selling professional services needs a different rhythm to a cafe on Instagram, so the plan is set against your business rather than a fixed template. The full scope sits on the social media service page.

faqs.q: Do I need to supply photos and content?
faqs.a: Send through whatever you already have: job site shots, product photos, your team, your premises. Your own images show your actual work in a way stock imagery cannot, so we would rather build from them. If you want professional photography or video production, that is a separate piece of work and we will say so rather than quietly padding the month with stock.

faqs.q: Which platform should my business be on?
faqs.a: Choose based on how your customers find and judge you, not on what is popular. Visual and consumer-facing work such as hospitality, retail, salons, fitness and trades tends to suit Instagram and Facebook, while selling to other businesses suits LinkedIn. One or two platforms run properly will beat five neglected accounts, so we will recommend a short list and explain the reasoning.
